pattern_description: COLLIDER (A → C ← B): Two independent causes A and B both influence a common effect C. A and B are unrelated in general, but conditioning on C creates a spurious negative association between them (collider bias / Berkson's paradox). Tests whether the LLM understands that conditioning on a collider can create misleading correlations.
difficulty_notes: Easy structurally (3 nodes, 2 edges) but conceptually tricky. Many models incorrectly assume a relationship between A and B because they share an effect.
description: Talent and luck both independently contribute to fame. Talent and luck are themselves unrelated.

explanation_points: Talent and Luck are independent causes of Fame
Fame is a collider — it is influenced by two unrelated factors
Talent does not cause Luck, and Luck does not cause Talent
Among famous people, you may observe a negative correlation between talent and luck (collider bias), but this is not a causal relationship

counterfactual.expected_outcome: Fame becomes more likely. Luck is unaffected because there is no causal arrow from Talent to Luck.
